Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding creating projects with `gcloud` and organization parent association:

## Key Findings:

**Creating Projects Without an Organization Parent:**
If "No organization" is an option, you can select it to create your new project as the top level of its own resource hierarchy.

**Automatic Association with Organization:**
By default, new projects belong to the organization associated with the user. Additionally, when a user with a Google Workspace or Cloud Identity account creates a Google Cloud project resource, an organization resource is automatically provisioned for them.

**Important Constraint for Managed Users:**
Managed users (members of the account domain) must create projects within an organization, and it's not possible for accounts associated with an organization resource to create project resources that aren't associated with an organization resource.

**Using gcloud Command:**
To create a project with an organization resource or a folder as parent, use the --organization or --folder flags.

**For Service Accounts:**
Service accounts are not allowed to create projects outside of an organization resource and must specify the parent resource when creating a project.

In summary, whether a project gets automatically associated with an organization depends on the account type creating it. Users with Google Workspace or Cloud Identity accounts will have their projects automatically associated with their organization by default.

content.text:
Decisive — and it kills the literal plan, in a useful way.

## The finding

**An identity that belongs to an organization cannot create a project outside that organization.** Straight from Google's docs:

> "It's not possible for accounts associated with an organization resource to create project resources that aren't associated with an organization resource... Managed users must create projects within an organization."
>
> "'No organization' ... is restricted to users without an existing organization resource."

So `bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` — a managed domain user — **cannot make a "No organization" project.** Anything it creates auto-parents under scaleinvariant.org and inherits `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation`. The literal ask ("use my org identity to set up a no-org pool") is the one impossible combination: org identity *forces* org projects.

## What that leaves — two real unblocks

| | (a) Org identity + in-org override | (b) Consumer gmail + no-org project |
|---|---|---|
| Payor identity | `bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org` | a personal gmail (no org) |
| How keys work | project-scoped policy override (memo §3, **proven**, you have the authority) | no constraint exists — RBRA just works |
| Reauth | yes — needs the session-policy lever (R4) | none (consumer tokens are immortal) |
| Override machinery | relevant again — ₢BSAAC/₢BSAAD/₢BSAAE earn their place | unneeded — drop them entirely |
| R3 fit | the "survival hack" R3 deprecated | **exactly R3's "RBRA = consumer/bootstrap mode"** |
| Cost | weakens your own org policy per-project | depot lives under a personal gmail, not your org — a detour from where it'll eventually live |

## My read

R3 actually points at **(b)**: if you want RBRA *now*, the coherent move is to run it on a *consumer* identity — not to force it onto your org with an override hack. (b) needs **zero** override paces and **zero** session-policy work — it sidesteps both problems the org created.

The catch is purely practical: (b) puts your bootstrap depot under a personal gmail rather than your org. Fine for an MVP/dogfooding depot you'll later rebuild under the org via federation; annoying if you want this depot to *be* the real one.

(a) keeps everything under your org identity but commits you to the override hack + the session-policy config — i.e., it *resurrects* ₢BSAAC/₢BSAAD/₢BSAAE as real work.

So the heat reshape now hinges on this single choice. Which fits how you actually want to work right now — **(b)** clean consumer bootstrap (drop the override paces), or **(a)** stay org-identity and productize/manual the override?

Sources:
- [Create projects — Resource Manager](https://docs.cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-projects)
- [Set up a Google Cloud organization resource](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-organization)

content.text:
Wait — that backstory reframes *everything*. Let me say the strategic thing first, then answer both questions.

## The reframe

The entire org-credential crisis — disableServiceAccountKeyCreation, the override hack, reauth, the keyless redesign — was triggered by a **mundane project-quota wall on gmail**, which you worked around by switching to an org account. The org dragged in all the secure-by-default constraints. **The org was an overcorrection for a quota problem.**

Which means the cheapest path back to "unblocked now" probably isn't (a) or (b) from my table — it's **fix the gmail quota and stay no-org.** No org, no override, no reauth, no federation. RBRA just works, exactly as R3's consumer/bootstrap tier intends. And it collapses most of this heat.

## Your two questions

**Q1 — new identity to farm projects?** Wrong tool, and I'd steer you off it:
- The **sanctioned, free fix is a project-quota increase request** (console → IAM & Admin → Quotas, or the "request more projects" link). That's the frown-free path.
- Your "so many deletion-pending projects" are the actual culprit — pending-deletion projects **count against your quota until they fully purge (~30 days)**, then free up automatically. So this is partly self-healing.
- A fresh identity to multiply free quota is circumvention-flavored (ToS discourages it), it multiplies your credential-management burden, and you'd just hit the same wall again. Quota bump or wait-it-out beats it on every axis.

**Q2 — same biller?** I can't observe that — you'd check in **console → Billing → your billing account → "Projects linked to this billing account"** (it'll list projects across *both* identities if they share one). Two Google identities *can* share a single billing account. But note: the **project-count limit is per-creating-identity, not per-billing-account** — so a shared biller doesn't change the count cap (it only pools your *spend*). The quota problem and the billing account are separate axes.

## Tie to the heat

If you bump the gmail quota (or wait out the purge), the unblock is "stay on no-org gmail," and ₣BS shrinks to roughly: keep ₢BSAAA (cleanup), drop ₢BSAAC/₢BSAAD/₢BSAAE (override machinery — moot), defer keyless to a future heat. The org becomes a *future federation* concern, not a *now* problem.

Want me to verify the current project-quota-increase mechanics (so you know exactly what to request), or is that clear enough to act on?

content.text:
Both questions answered, and they converge on something important.

## Same biller? **No.**

The gmail billing account `0173BC-6A77FA-3796BC` is linked to exactly **three** projects — and they're the same three gmail active projects (`rbwg-p-251228075220` = your gmail Payor project, plus two `canest` test depots). Since gmail *owns* that billing account, it would see *any* project riding it regardless of identity. The org depot isn't there. **So your two identities are not sharing a billing account** — the org depot is on a different one.

## The convergence (this is the real finding)

Three things you've been treating as separate are **one root issue**:

- The gmail **quota wall** → 72 pending-deletion projects
- The **org move** that triggered the whole credential crisis
- The §11 **"gauntlet coupling"** open item

The 72 corpses are gauntlet/proof depots — the full gauntlet levies a *fresh depot per run* and leaves them to a 30-day purge. That churn filled gmail's quota → you moved to the org to get headroom → the org's `disableServiceAccountKeyCreation` triggered the tectonic redesign. **The gauntlet's depot churn is the upstream cause of everything in this heat.**

## So, your questions answered straight

- **New identity to farm projects?** Don't — and it wouldn't even fix this. The problem isn't account count, it's 72 corpses clogging one quota; a fresh identity just gives you a new quota to refill on the next gauntlet run. (Also circumvention-flavored, as discussed.)
- **The actual fix** is two mundane moves: **request a project-quota increase** (instant, free, sanctioned) *or* wait ~days for the oldest to purge; **and** stop the gauntlet leaving corpses — the §11 "lean on skirmish / standing depot" answer.

## Heat implication

This strongly suggests the **org-credential work was an overcorrection.** For MVP you can likely stay **no-org on gmail**, which means:
- Drop ₢BSAAC / ₢BSAAD / ₢BSAAE (override machinery) — moot with no org.
- The real near-term work is **gauntlet depot hygiene** (so quota doesn't refill), which may belong in a testing heat, not here.
- Org / keyless / federation → genuinely future (roadmap entry already placed).
- ₢BSAAF → wrap.

Want me to bank this convergence as a resolution (R4: "org move was an overcorrection for gauntlet-driven quota exhaustion; MVP stays no-org; fix is quota-increase + gauntlet depot hygiene"), then we reshape the heat around it?

content.text:
**No — nothing has been reaped yet.**

- **Oldest pending-deletion project** (`prlc-d-pristq100000`, delete-requested 2026-04-27) is **still `DELETE_REQUESTED`**. Since the purge takes the oldest first and that one is still present, the 30-day timer hasn't fired for *any* of them. First reaps land **~May 27–28** (Apr 27 + 30 days), then a slow daily drip after.
- The sequence gap (`canest2bhl100010`) returns permission-denied/not-found — but it would date to mid-May, well *inside* the 30-day window, so it's not a timer reap; almost certainly a failed/aborted creation, not relevant to quota recovery.

**Implication:** quota relief is ~4+ days out and then drains slowly. So if you want to work *now*, waiting won't cut it — you'd want the **project-quota-increase request** (instant, free, sanctioned) for immediate headroom.
